We Did Not Win a Darwin Award Tales of Survival, Stupidity, and Second Chances Preface What This Book Is About A Note on the Darwin Awards How to Read This Book A Word of Caution Contributing Your Story

We Did Not Win a Darwin Award Tales of Survival, Stupidity, and Second Chances Edited by [Your Name] 2026-04-14 Preface “I have survived many things that should have killed me. I am either very lucky or evolution is still deciding.” - Anonymous Contributor What This Book Is About The Darwin Awards, for those unfamiliar, represent a darkly humorous tribute to those who improve the human gene pool by accidentally removing themselves from it through acts of spectacular stupidity. Since the 1990s, these stories have been collected, shared, and grimly celebrated across the internet and in bestselling books. But what about the rest of us? What about those moments when we stood at the precipice of our own Darwin Award moment and somehow—through luck, divine intervention, or a last-second burst of common sense—managed to step back? What about the stories that begin with “I can’t believe I’m still alive” rather than ending with “They never found the body?” This book is a collection of those stories. We Did Not Win a Darwin Award celebrates the near-misses, the close calls, and the “what was I thinking?” moments that we survived to tell about. These are true stories from real people who, for one reason or another, should probably not be here to share their tales. Some are funny. Some are terrifying. Some are both. Each chapter in this collection represents a different way to almost earn evolution’s most dubious honor—from misadventures with fire and explosives to ill-advised encounters with wildlife, from gravity-defying stupidity to chemistry experiments gone wrong. A Note on the Darwin Awards The concept of the Darwin Award emerged from Usenet newsgroups in the 1980s and was formalized by Wendy Northcutt in 1994 with the creation of DarwinAwards.com. The awards are named after Charles Darwin, whose theory of natural selection suggests that advantageous traits are passed on to future generations. The Darwin Awards, in their morbid way, celebrate “de-selection”—the removal of disadvantageous traits (like catastrophic stupidity) from the gene pool. To “win” a Darwin Award, one must: Die or be sterilized (removal from the gene pool) Demonstrate excellent stupidity Be the sole cause of one’s own demise Be a mature, capable adult Have the event verified as true The people in this book met criteria #2 through #4, but fortunately failed at #1. We survived our stupidity. We lived to tell the tale. We did NOT win a Darwin Award. And we’re strangely proud of it. How to Read This Book Each chapter is themed around a particular category of near-disaster. Some chapters feature a single, in-depth story of one person’s remarkable survival. Others collect multiple shorter tales united by theme—“Fire and Explosives,” “Gravity: Not Just a Good Idea,” “When Animals Attack Back,” and so on. The stories are contributed by people from all walks of life. Some contributors chose to use their real names; others preferred pseudonyms to protect the innocent (or not-so-innocent). Details have been changed in some stories to protect privacy, but the essential truth—and the essential foolishness—remains intact. Feel free to read this book front to back, or jump to whatever category of stupidity interests you most. We won’t judge. After all, we’re hardly in a position to judge anyone. A Word of Caution Do not try any of the things described in this book. We survived. You might not be so lucky. The stories collected here are not instruction manuals or dares. They are cautionary tales wrapped in humor and hindsight. We share them not to inspire imitation, but to inspire reflection—and perhaps a chuckle at the absurdity of human behavior when common sense takes a holiday. Contributing Your Story This book is a living project. If you have a story of how you narrowly avoided a Darwin Award, we’d love to hear it. Visit [website/contact information] to submit your tale of survival and stupidity. Remember: If you’re reading this, you survived. And that makes you part of an exclusive club—people too lucky, too stubborn, or too accidentally brilliant to win evolution’s booby prize. Welcome to the survivors’ circle.

Chapter 1 Fire and Explosives 1.1 Introduction 1.2 The Gasoline Incident: A Cautionary Tale 1.3 The Chemistry Set Chronicles 1.4 Short Takes: Quick Paths to Immolation 1.5 Conclusion: Playing with Fire

Chapter 1 Fire and Explosives “The difference between science and screwing around is writing it down.” - Adam Savage (who presumably survived his experiments) 1.1 Introduction There’s something primal about fire. It draws us in, fascinates us, makes us think we can control forces we barely understand. Add in the human tendency to mix things that shouldn’t be mixed, and you have a recipe for disaster—or at least singed eyebrows and missing digits. This chapter collects stories from people who learned the hard way that fire is not their friend, explosives are not toys, and “What’s the worst that could happen?” is not a valid safety protocol. 1.2 The Gasoline Incident: A Cautionary Tale Contributor: Michael T. (Real name, because “everyone in my hometown already knows this story”) I was sixteen, which is already a risky age for decision-making. It was a hot summer day in rural Montana, the kind where you can see heat waves rising off the pavement and every teenage boy is looking for something—anything—to do. My friend Jake and I had been tasked with clearing brush from behind his dad’s barn. This involved a lot of dried grass, tumbleweeds, and the accumulated plant matter of several neglectful years. Jake’s dad, in his infinite wisdom (sarcasm noted in retrospect), told us we could “just burn it” to make the job easier. He gave us matches. He did not, however, give us detailed instructions on the proper use of accelerants. We piled up the brush into what we thought was an impressive mound—probably eight feet high and twice as wide. We tried lighting it with matches. Nothing. The outer layer was too dry and the inside too dense. We tried newspaper. Still nothing that resembled the satisfying bonfire we’d envisioned. “We need gasoline,” Jake declared, with the confidence of someone who had never been on fire. I should have objected. I didn’t. Jake returned from the barn with a five-gallon gas can, nearly full. We proceeded to pour it over the brush pile—not a little, not a measured amount, but generous glugs of gasoline that soaked into the dried vegetation and pooled on the ground beneath. The smell should have warned us. The fumes should have been a giant red flag. We were standing in a cloud of vaporized gasoline on a 95-degree day, about to introduce an open flame. I struck the match. I don’t remember throwing it. I don’t remember the first moment of ignition. What I remember is the WHOOMP—a sound that’s not quite an explosion but not quite not an explosion—and suddenly being on my back ten feet away with no eyebrows and the distinct smell of burnt hair. The fireball that erupted from that brush pile was visible from the highway, half a mile away. It mushroomed up into a column that had to be thirty feet high. The heat was so intense that the paint on the side of the barn started bubbling. Dried grass in a twenty-foot radius around the pile ignited spontaneously. Jake’s dad came running out of the house screaming words I cannot reproduce in print. The volunteer fire department showed up. So did Jake’s mom. So did, eventually, a very unimpressed sheriff’s deputy who lectured us on reckless endangerment while I stood there, eyebrow-less and reeking of singed hair. The brush pile burned for six hours. The barn survived, barely. My pride did not. 1.2.1 What I Learned Gasoline vapor ignites. You don’t need to put fire directly on liquid gasoline—the fumes alone will explode. A “little” gasoline is a dangerous amount. Five gallons is criminally stupid. Eyebrows grow back, but slowly, and you look very weird without them. When adults say “be careful with fire,” they mean it. The distance you think you need to stand back is not nearly far enough. I did not win a Darwin Award that day, but I came close enough to see it waving from across the finish line. I’m 34 now, and I still check twice before lighting anything more dangerous than a birthday candle. 1.3 The Chemistry Set Chronicles Contributor: Anonymous (for obvious reasons) [Editor’s note: This contributor requested anonymity due to the statute of limitations being unclear on some of these activities.] When I was twelve, I received a chemistry set for my birthday. Not one of those sad modern ones with five pre-measured experiments—this was the early 1990s, and my set came with actual chemicals, glass tubes, and a disturbing lack of adult supervision. What follows is a brief list of things I should not have done: 1.3.1 Experiment 1: The Tennis Ball Bomb I learned that you could cut open a tennis ball, fill it with match heads scraped from several dozen matches, seal it back up with duct tape, and throw it against a hard surface for a satisfying BANG. What I did not consider: doing this in my bedroom with wooden floors. The scorch mark remained until my parents sold that house ten years later. 1.3.2 Experiment 2: Dry Ice and Plastic Bottles Dry ice in a plastic bottle with water creates pressure. Enough pressure, and the bottle explodes. I knew this intellectually. What I didn’t calculate was the shrapnel potential of a two-liter soda bottle. The bottle exploded while I was holding it. I had plastic shards embedded in my jacket. If I hadn’t been wearing thick winter clothing because I was doing this in the garage in January, I would have been shredded. My hearing rang for three days. 1.3.3 Experiment 3: The Chlorine Incident Chemistry sets used to come with little packets of various substances. I discovered that mixing certain pool chemicals with other substances from under the kitchen sink created chlorine gas. I did this in a enclosed space (my bedroom, again). I gave myself chlorine gas poisoning and had to be taken to the ER, where I lied and said I’d been cleaning the bathroom and “mixed things by accident.” My mother believed me because the alternative—that her twelve-year-old was conducting chemical warfare experiments in his bedroom—was too terrible to contemplate. 1.3.4 Lessons Learned Chemistry sets are educational. What they teach you is how close you can come to killing yourself. Ventilation is not optional. If you’re hiding what you’re doing from adults, there’s probably a good reason they wouldn’t approve. The ER doctors know you’re lying, but they’re too professional to say so. I’m a chemical engineer now, which my mother finds either ironic or proof that I learned something. I work in safety protocols for industrial manufacturing. My colleagues would be horrified to know my origin story. 1.4 Short Takes: Quick Paths to Immolation 1.4.1 The Hairspray Flamethrower Contributor: Sarah K. Aerosol + lighter = flamethrower. This is known by every teenager in history. What is less known: hairspray flamethrowers melt plastic shower curtains incredibly fast, and flaming plastic drips. I have a scar on my forearm twenty years later. 1.4.2 The Microwave Armageddon Contributor: David R. In college, we discovered that putting Ivory soap in the microwave makes it expand into a massive cloud. Then we discovered that metal in the microwave makes lightning. Then we discovered that trying both simultaneously makes the microwave explode, sets off the fire alarm, evacuates the dorm, and gets you banned from having kitchen privileges for a semester. 1.4.3 The Firework Mishap Contributor: Jennifer L. Roman candles are meant to shoot flaming balls into the air. They are not meant to be held backwards. I learned the difference at a Fourth of July party. My garage did not appreciate the lesson. Neither did the fire department. 1.4.4 The Lighter Fluid Incident Contributor: Thomas W. When a charcoal grill won’t light, the solution is not to pour an entire bottle of lighter fluid on it and then toss a match from a “safe distance.” The fireball singed my entire front yard and melted my flip-flops while they were still on my feet. 1.5 Conclusion: Playing with Fire Fire is humanity’s oldest tool and our most persistent danger. We’ve been burning ourselves since we discovered how to create flames, and apparently, we haven’t learned much in the intervening millennia. The stories in this chapter share common themes: Underestimating combustion: Fire is faster, hotter, and more aggressive than we think Overconfidence: “I know what I’m doing” is rarely true Inadequate safety margins: We stand too close, use too much accelerant, and skip safety equipment Luck: Most of us survived because of factors beyond our control If there’s wisdom to be found in these tales, it’s this: Respect fire. Respect chemistry. And when that little voice in your head says “This might be a bad idea,” listen to it. That voice is the only thing standing between you and a Darwin Award nomination. Stay safe. Stay smart. And for the love of all that’s holy, please don’t try any of this at home. Have your own fire-and-explosives near-miss story? We’d love to hear it (assuming you survived to tell it). Contact us at [submission information].

Chapter 2 Gravity Lessons The Physics of Falling 2.1 “The Roof Wasn’t As Close As It Looked” 2.2 “Parkour Is Not For Everyone” Collected Short Tales: When Gravity Reminded Us Reflections on Gravity The Science of Falling Editor’s Note

Chapter 2 Gravity Lessons “What goes up must come down. The question is whether you come down in one piece.” - Newton’s Lesser-Known Corollary Gravity is perhaps the most consistent force in our lives. It never takes a day off. It never forgets. It never makes exceptions for stupidity or hubris. And yet, time and time again, human beings seem to forget that gravity is not just a good idea—it’s the law. This chapter collects stories from people who tested gravity’s patience and somehow lived to regret it. The Physics of Falling Before we dive into these tales, let’s establish some facts: Acceleration due to gravity: 9.8 m/s² Terminal velocity for a human: ~120 mph (53 m/s) in a spread-eagle position Survivable fall height: Highly variable, but anything over 20 feet is considered “serious” Number of times gravity has lost: 0 With that cheerful information in mind, meet our survivors. 2.1 “The Roof Wasn’t As Close As It Looked” Contributor: Jake M., California Age at incident: 19 Height of fall: ~25 feet Injuries: Fractured ankle, bruised ego, lifetime ban from apartment complex I was a sophomore in college, and my buddies and I had decided that the best way to get to the roof of our apartment building for a party was not through the boring internal stairwell, but by climbing up the outside. Yes, you read that right. We had roof access. Legal, safe, boring roof access via stairs. But where’s the fun in that? The building was three stories, and there were decorative concrete ledges every floor. My friend Chad made it look easy—he practically scampered up like a drunk, overconfident squirrel. So naturally, I figured I could do the same. The first two floors went fine. I was feeling invincible. I was feeling cool. I was feeling like Spider-Man, if Spider-Man had consumed six beers and had zero superpowers. On the third ledge, about 25 feet up, I made a critical error. I looked down. Now, looking down wouldn’t have been a problem if I hadn’t suddenly realized how incredibly stupid this was. My hands got sweaty. My confidence evaporated. And I did what any rational person would do in that situation—I panicked and tried to jump the last few feet to the roof. I missed. More specifically, I got my hands on the roof edge, but my feet found nothing but air. For a moment, I hung there, and I actually had time to think: “This is how I die. They’re going to put this in the campus newspaper. My mom is going to kill me. Wait, the fall will kill me first.” Then my grip failed. Here’s where I got lucky: I didn’t fall straight down. I bounced off the ledge I’d been standing on, which changed my angle. Instead of hitting the concrete patio below, I crashed through the branches of a medium-sized tree and landed in the decorative bark mulch of the landscaping. The tree absolutely destroyed me—branches whipped my face, scraped my arms, and one particularly aggressive limb caught me right in the ribs. But it also slowed my fall enough that when I hit the ground, I only fractured my ankle instead of shattering every bone in my body. I lay there in the mulch, staring up at the stars, listening to my friends screaming from the roof, and realized I’d just used up probably a decade’s worth of luck in about five seconds. The resident advisor found me there. The paramedics came. My parents were called. I was banned from the apartment complex (though I was graduating that year anyway). But I was alive. Lesson learned: Stairs exist for a reason. Also, plant more trees—they save idiots like me. 2.2 “Parkour Is Not For Everyone” Contributor: Anonymous, UK Age at incident: 22 Height of fall: ~12 feet Injuries: Concussion, shattered wrist, demolished pride The year was 2009, and parkour was everywhere on YouTube. Every other video showed impossibly athletic people leaping between buildings, vaulting over railings, and generally making it look like human beings were meant to fly. I watched these videos religiously. I was in decent shape. I’d done gymnastics as a kid. I thought, “How hard can it be?” The answer: Very hard. My first mistake was not starting small. My second mistake was choosing an urban location with concrete surfaces. My third mistake was doing this alone, without telling anyone where I was going. I’d picked a spot between two commercial buildings where there was about a six-foot gap between rooftops, both about twelve feet high. The parkour videos made this look trivial—you just run and jump, land in a roll, done. I climbed up to the first roof using a dumpster and a drainpipe (mistake number four). The rooftop was flat, graveled, and seemed solid enough. I backed up to get a running start, psyched myself up, and sprinted toward the edge. Here’s what I didn’t account for: 1. Gravel provides terrible traction for a running start 2. The gap was actually closer to eight feet, not six 3. The far roof was slightly lower, changing the angle 4. I’d never actually practiced a running jump of any distance 5. Fear kicks in right at the edge I launched myself into the air with all the grace of a drunk penguin. For a glorious half-second, I thought I might make it. I could see the other roof. I could see where I’d land. I could almost feel the triumph. Then physics kicked in. I didn’t make it. Not even close. My hands barely grasped the edge of the far roof, my body slammed into the brick wall, and I fell straight down into the alley below. I landed on my left side, my arm outstretched to break the fall (which it did—by shattering my wrist). My head bounced off the concrete. The world went white, then dark, then swirly. I don’t know how long I lay there. Could have been seconds, could have been minutes. When I came to, I was staring up at a narrow slice of sky between buildings, my arm was screaming at me, and my head felt like it was full of angry bees. I tried to move. Bad idea. Everything hurt. My wrist was bent at an angle that wrists should not bend. I tasted blood. I might have vomited—the memory is fuzzy. Eventually, I managed to pull out my phone with my good hand and call 999. The ambulance took forty minutes to find me because I couldn’t clearly explain where I was. The paramedics asked what happened, and I mumbled something about “fell off a wall.” The diagnosis: Concussion, fractured wrist in three places, bruised ribs, various lacerations, and severely damaged pride. The hospital staff were professional enough not to laugh, but I saw the looks they exchanged. Recovery took months. I had to have surgery on my wrist with pins and plates. I still can’t rotate it fully. And every time it rains, it aches—a permanent reminder of the day I thought I was Spider-Man. Lessons learned: - YouTube skills are not transferable to real life - Professional parkour practitioners train for years and still get hurt - Always tell someone where you’re going - If you’re going to do something stupid, at least start small - Gravity doesn’t care about your confidence level I did not win a Darwin Award, but I came remarkably close. The doctor said if I’d landed a few inches differently, I could have cracked my skull or broken my neck. These days, I stick to the stairs. Collected Short Tales: When Gravity Reminded Us 2.2.1 The Ladder Incident Submitted by Robert T., Texas “Ladders are only safe if you use them correctly. I learned this while cleaning gutters and decided to ‘just lean a little farther’ rather than climb down and move the ladder. Spoiler: I moved faster than the ladder did. Three broken ribs and a new respect for proper ladder safety protocols.” 2.2.2 The Hammock Failure Submitted by Maria V., Oregon “I installed a hammock between two trees in my backyard. What I didn’t account for was the weight rating, the quality of the rope, or the basic laws of physics. I climbed in with my girlfriend, a cooler of drinks, and my dog. The combined weight was approximately 350 pounds. The hammock was rated for 200. We fell six feet onto the ground. The dog landed on his feet. We did not.” 2.2.3 The Zipline Miscalculation Submitted by Chris P., Colorado “Built a backyard zipline from my deck to a tree. Used hardware store parts and YouTube instructions. Did not properly calculate the tension, the angle, or the stopping mechanism. First ride ended with me slamming into the tree at about 15 mph. Separated shoulder, black eye from a branch, and a wife who said ‘I told you so’ for the next six months.” 2.2.4 The Balcony Shortcut Submitted by Tyler K., Florida “Spring Break, hotel room on the third floor, locked myself out on the balcony. Brilliant idea: climb over to my buddy’s balcony next door. It was only about four feet across. I got halfway over, hanging from the railing, when I realized I couldn’t reach the other side. Hung there for what felt like forever before my arms gave out. Fell into the pool below by sheer luck. Could’ve easily been the pavement.” 2.2.5 The Tree Climbing Lesson Submitted by Ashley M., North Carolina “Age 30. Climbed a tree to rescue my cat. The cat came down on its own after ten minutes. I was stuck for an hour because I’d climbed higher than I realized and was suddenly terrified. Fire department had to come get me. The cat judged me from the ground. My neighbors still bring it up at parties.” Reflections on Gravity What is it about human nature that makes us continually underestimate gravity? Perhaps it’s our bipedal evolution—we spent so long learning to stand upright that we forgot how easy it is to fall down. Perhaps it’s the illusion of control—we feel like we’re choosing to be up high, so surely we can choose not to fall. Or perhaps we’re just idiots sometimes. Every person in this chapter survived their encounter with gravity, but not without cost. Broken bones, scars, medical bills, and the eternal knowledge that gravity was there, waiting patiently for us to screw up. The survivors in this chapter share a common trait: they all had a moment of clarity mid-fall where they realized the magnitude of their error. And they all had just enough luck—a tree, a pile of snow, a fortuitously placed awning, a pool—to give them a second chance. Gravity doesn’t usually give second chances. They won’t make the same mistake twice. They’ve learned their lesson. They did not win a Darwin Award. But it was closer than any of them would care to admit. The Science of Falling For those interested in the physics of why these stories exist: Free fall acceleration: Objects fall at 9.8 m/s², meaning your speed increases by about 22 mph every second you’re falling. Distance and velocity: - 10 feet: ~17 mph impact - 20 feet: ~25 mph impact - 30 feet: ~30 mph impact - 50 feet: ~39 mph impact Survivability: Highly variable depending on: - Landing surface (water, grass, concrete, snow) - Body position on impact - Age and physical condition - Sheer dumb luck Most survivable falls: People have survived falls from airplanes (with extraordinary circumstances). The record is over 30,000 feet. Least survivable falls: Falls from as little as 6-10 feet can be fatal if you land wrong, especially headfirst onto concrete. The lesson? Height matters, but how you fall and where you land matter just as much. Editor’s Note If you have a story about surviving a fall, a jump, or any other gravity-related near-disaster, please submit it to [contact information]. We’re particularly interested in stories involving: Roof access gone wrong Tree climbing miscalculations Balcony incidents Rock climbing without proper equipment “I can make that jump” moments Ladder failures Construction site mishaps Any other vertical misadventures Remember: We’re looking for stories where you survived. If you didn’t survive, you won’t be able to submit anyway. Stay grounded, everyone. [Additional stories to be collected and added in future editions]

Chapter 3 Electricity and Water The Backyard Pool Incident Tales from the Bathroom The Hot Tub Electrician The Aquarium Heater Incident Reflections on Voltage and Regret

Chapter 3 Electricity and Water “Water and electricity: The universe’s way of testing whether you paid attention in physics class.” - Murphy’s Law, Extended Edition Somewhere in the collective unconscious of humanity, we all know that electricity and water don’t mix. We’re told this from childhood. It’s in every movie, every safety manual, every warning label. And yet, with remarkable consistency, people find creative ways to ignore this fundamental truth. This chapter collects stories from those who learned—the hard way—that H₂O and high voltage are not friends. The Backyard Pool Incident Contributed by Marcus T., Phoenix, Arizona I was twenty-three and had just bought my first house. It came with a pool, which I thought was the height of sophistication. What I didn’t have was money for a pool service, so I decided to maintain it myself. How hard could it be? The filter pump had been making a weird noise, and after extensive research (by which I mean I watched one YouTube video), I determined it needed cleaning. The problem was that the pump was partially submerged in water from a recent rain that had flooded the equipment area. “I’ll just unplug it,” I thought, wading into the ankle-deep water in flip-flops. Here’s what I didn’t know: The pump was hardwired. There was no plug. The switch was inside the garage, which I hadn’t bothered to check. I reached down to fiddle with what I thought was a connection point. The moment my hand touched the metal housing, every muscle in my body locked up. I couldn’t let go. I couldn’t scream. I couldn’t move. Time seemed to slow down as electricity coursed through me, and all I could think was, “This is the stupidest way to die.” My neighbor, Miguel, who happened to be walking by, saw me through the fence. He didn’t touch me—smart man—but he ran to my garage and started flipping every breaker until one of them cut the power. I collapsed into the water, gasping, my heart doing a drum solo. Miguel called 911. The paramedics checked me over, lectured me extensively, and confirmed I’d live. The ER doctor later told me I was incredibly lucky—the current could easily have stopped my heart. The electrician I hired to properly fix everything (after I got out of the hospital) asked me what I’d been thinking. I didn’t have a good answer then, and I don’t have one now. I no longer own a pool. I live in an apartment. I don’t even change light bulbs without triple-checking they’re off. Lesson learned: Electricity doesn’t care about your YouTube education. Hire professionals. Tales from the Bathroom The Hair Dryer Chronicles Contributed by Anonymous, Toronto, Canada Look, I KNOW you’re not supposed to use a hair dryer near water. Everyone knows that. There are warning labels. There are PSAs. There was probably a very special episode of some TV show about it. But I was running late, my hair was wet, and I’d just gotten into the bathtub to shave my legs because the lighting was better there. The hair dryer was on the counter, plugged in from that morning. I thought, “I’ll just reach over and grab it for a second. I’ll be careful.” The cord caught on the shampoo bottle. The hair dryer tumbled toward the tub in slow motion, like something from an action movie. I watched it fall, still running, directly toward the water I was sitting in. I moved faster than I’ve ever moved in my life. I launched myself backward, hit my head on the tile, and somehow managed to catch the dryer one-handed before it hit the water. I fumbled it, bobbled it, and finally gripped it with both hands, holding it above my head like Simba in The Lion King, my heart trying to escape my chest. For about thirty seconds, I just sat there, dripping, holding the hair dryer, having a small existential crisis about my life choices. Then I unplugged it, got out of the tub, sat on the floor, and cried a little. I now dry my hair in the bedroom. The bathroom outlet has a GFCI. I have therapy appointments. Lesson learned: Multitasking has limits. Those limits include electricity and bathtubs. The Bathroom Radio Contributed by James K., Liverpool, UK In my defense, it was the ’90s, and we didn’t have phones to bring into the bathroom for entertainment. What we had was a small radio that sat on the counter, and I loved taking long baths while listening to the Sunday evening request show. One evening, the radio reception got fuzzy. I reached over to adjust the antenna—which extended out over the tub—while I was still in the water. The radio’s position on the counter wasn’t stable, and it wobbled. I tried to catch it. I should not have tried to catch it. My wet hand grabbed the radio just as it tipped into the water. For a fraction of a second, I felt the jolt start to run through me. Then—miracle of miracles—the plug popped out of the wall socket. The whole thing had been barely plugged in, and my jerking motion to catch the radio pulled it free. I sat there in the tub with a dead radio in my hand, water sloshing everywhere, my life having flashed before my eyes. The next day, I bought a waterproof Bluetooth speaker. Twenty-five years later, I still get anxious when electronics are anywhere near the bathroom. Lesson learned: Bad reception isn’t worth dying for. Also, check your plugs. The Hot Tub Electrician Contributed by Dave R., Seattle, Washington I work in IT, which means I’m legally required to believe I can fix anything electronic. This confidence does not always serve me well. We had a hot tub in the backyard that kept tripping the breaker. I decided to troubleshoot it myself. I should mention that it was raining—classic Seattle weather—and the hot tub was full of water. Obviously. I opened the electrical panel on the side of the tub. I saw a loose wire. I thought, “I’ll just tighten that connection real quick.” Did I turn off the breaker first? Reader, I did not. Did I consider that I was standing in a puddle while wearing wet shoes? Also no. I reached in with a screwdriver—metal handle, naturally—and touched the live connection. The shock threw me backward. I landed on my ass in the puddle, seeing stars. My arm felt like I’d punched a hornet’s nest from the inside. My wife came running out, took one look at me sprawled in the mud, and said, “Did you turn off the power first?” “No,” I wheezed. “Idiot,” she said, which was fair. She called an actual electrician, who fixed the problem in ten minutes and charged us $150. He told me that if the current had gone across my chest instead of down my arm, I’d probably be dead. I stick to IT now. If it doesn’t have a keyboard, I don’t touch it. Lesson learned: Your degree in one field doesn’t grant you magical powers in another. Also, breakers exist for a reason. The Aquarium Heater Incident Contributed by Sarah L., Austin, Texas I had a 75-gallon saltwater aquarium that was my pride and joy. The heater died one day, and my fish were getting cold. I ordered a replacement on Amazon, but it wouldn’t arrive for two days. I couldn’t let my expensive fish suffer. So I had a brilliant idea: I’d use a space heater to warm the room. Then I had an even more brilliant idea: What if I just put the space heater close to the aquarium? Then I had my most brilliant idea yet: What if I suspended the space heater OVER the open-top aquarium to warm the water directly? I rigged up this elaborate system with some wire and a chair. The space heater dangled about six inches above the water, blowing hot air down onto the surface. I went to make coffee. When I came back five minutes later, the wire had slipped. The space heater was now hanging at an angle, its bottom edge about two inches from the water’s surface. It was swaying slightly. I watched in horror as it swung just a little too far and the corner dipped into the water. There was a loud POP, a flash of light, and every fish in my tank simultaneously freaked out. The breaker tripped. The room went dark. I stood there in silence, contemplating my choices. Miraculously, the fish all survived. The space heater did not. Neither did the breaker, which had to be replaced. The electrician asked me what happened, and I lied because I was too embarrassed to tell him the truth. Lesson learned: Patience is a virtue. Amazon two-day shipping is faster than fish funeral planning. Reflections on Voltage and Regret The common thread in all these stories is simple: We knew better. Every single one of us had been taught that electricity and water are a dangerous combination. But in the moment—rushed, distracted, overconfident—we ignored what we knew. Electricity is invisible, which makes it easy to underestimate. Water is familiar, which makes it easy to grow complacent around. Together, they’re a combination that can kill in seconds. We’re lucky. We’re alive to tell these stories, to laugh nervously about them, to share them as cautionary tales. But luck runs out. Physics doesn’t negotiate. If there’s one thing this chapter should teach you, it’s this: That voice in your head saying, “This is probably a bad idea?” That voice is correct. Listen to it. And for the love of all that is holy, turn off the breaker first. Key Takeaways: Water + Electricity = Darwin Award nomination GFCIs (Ground Fault Circuit Interrupters) save lives—install them in bathrooms and near water “I’ll just be careful” is not a safety plan If you don’t know what you’re doing with electricity, hire someone who does Wet hands, wet floors, and wet anything else should mean “do not touch electrical equipment” Your overconfidence is not an adequate substitute for training Breakers exist for a reason—use them We survived our encounters with electricity and water, but the universe gave us a warning. We’re passing that warning on to you. Don’t make our mistakes. Your fish, your hair, and your family will thank you.
vehicles.html

Chapter 4 Vehicle Misadventures 4.1 Introduction 4.2 The Highway Learning Experience 4.3 Parking Lot Physics 4.4 The Motorcycle Chronicles 4.5 Teenage Driver Tales 4.6 When GPS Goes Wrong 4.7 The Towing Incident 4.8 Racing and Recklessness 4.9 Short Takes: Quick Trips to the Edge 4.10 Reflections on Vehicles and Velocity Editor’s Note

Chapter 4 Vehicle Misadventures “The difference between a car and a coffin is sometimes just good brakes and better judgment.” - Anonymous Traffic Safety Instructor 4.1 Introduction Humans have been trying to kill themselves with vehicles since the invention of the wheel. We drive too fast, we drive too distracted, we drive under the influence of substances both legal and illegal, and we make split-second decisions that should absolutely result in our removal from the gene pool. And yet, somehow, many of us survive. This chapter collects stories from people who treated traffic laws as suggestions, physics as a negotiable concept, and common sense as optional equipment. They lived to tell their tales. Barely. 4.2 The Highway Learning Experience Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Story to be collected from contributors about near-miss highway incidents, wrong-way driving, falling asleep at the wheel, or other highway-related close calls] Placeholder for contributed story: The narrative arc should include: - The setup: Why were they on the road? - The decision: What choice led to the near-disaster? - The incident: What actually happened? - The outcome: How did they survive? - The lesson: What did they learn? 4.3 Parking Lot Physics Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Story to be collected about parking garage incidents, parking brake failures, or other parking-related misadventures] 4.4 The Motorcycle Chronicles Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Story to be collected from motorcycle riders about near-death experiences, crashes avoided at the last second, or ill-advised stunts] Motorcycles are statistically dangerous enough without human stupidity added to the equation. The contributors to this section learned that: Wheelies are harder than they look Road rash is exactly as painful as it sounds “I saw it in a movie” is not a valid reason to attempt a stunt Protective gear exists for a reason 4.5 Teenage Driver Tales Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Stories to be collected about new drivers, first cars, and the special kind of stupidity that comes with being 16 and having a driver’s license] The teenage brain is not fully developed, particularly the part responsible for risk assessment. Combine that with: - Peer pressure - A false sense of invincibility - Limited driving experience - Access to a vehicle And you have a recipe for disaster. Or, in our contributors’ cases, a recipe for almost-disaster. 4.6 When GPS Goes Wrong Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Modern stories about blindly following GPS into lakes, off cliffs, down stairs, or other locations that are clearly not roads] 4.7 The Towing Incident Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Stories about improper towing, homemade towing solutions, or towing gone catastrophically wrong] 4.8 Racing and Recklessness Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Stories about street racing, drag racing on public roads, or trying to recreate Fast & Furious scenes in real life] 4.9 Short Takes: Quick Trips to the Edge 4.9.1 The Ice Incident Contributed by [Name - To Be Collected] [Story about driving on ice, black ice, or winter conditions without proper preparation] 4.9.2 The Flood Contributed by [Name - To Be Collected] [Story about driving through flood water, getting stranded, or underestimating water depth] 4.9.3 The Deer Contributed by [Name - To Be Collected] [Story about wildlife collisions or near-collisions] 4.9.4 The Texting Incident Contributed by [Name - To Be Collected] [Story about distracted driving and its nearly-fatal consequences] 4.10 Reflections on Vehicles and Velocity Vehicles give us incredible power—the power to travel at speeds our bodies were never designed to handle, to transport tons of cargo, to traverse vast distances. With that power comes responsibility, and too often, we forget that. The survivors in this chapter all share similar realizations: Speed kills: What feels fast and fun can become deadly in seconds Distraction is deadly: Taking your eyes off the road for “just a second” is enough Vehicles are weapons: A car can kill as easily as a gun Physics always wins: You can’t brake your way out of certain situations Luck runs out: Every near-miss is borrowed time We were all fortunate. We all had that moment where time slowed down, where we realized we’d made a terrible mistake, where we thought, “This is it.” And then, through some combination of luck, skill, and divine intervention, we walked away. We didn’t win a Darwin Award that day. But we came close enough to see it in the rearview mirror. Editor’s Note This chapter is actively seeking contributions. If you have a story about: Car crashes you shouldn’t have survived Motorcycle incidents with miraculous outcomes Driving under the influence (and the consequences) Road rage incidents that escalated dangerously Mechanical failures at inopportune moments Learning to drive (badly) Stunt attempts gone wrong Any other vehicle-related near-death experience Please submit your story to [contact information]. We’re particularly interested in stories that include: - Specific details about what went wrong - The moment of realization - How you survived - What you learned - How it changed your behavior Remember: If you’re reading this, you survived. Share your story so others might learn from your mistakes. Drive safe, everyone. The Darwin Awards can wait. Content for this chapter is being actively collected. Check back for updates as contributors share their stories of vehicular near-misses and mechanical mishaps.
wildlife.html

Chapter 5 Wildlife Encounters 5.1 Introduction 5.2 The Bear That Said No 5.3 The Snake Incident 5.4 Sharks, Jellyfish, and Ocean Regrets 5.5 The Kangaroo Boxing Match (Extended) 5.6 Insects That Fight Back 5.7 The Moose Incident (Continued) 5.8 When Pets Turn Wild 5.9 Bird Attacks 5.10 The Alligator Misunderstanding 5.11 Spiders and Other Arachnids 5.12 Short Takes: Brief Wildlife Regrets 5.13 Lessons from the Animal Kingdom 5.14 Statistical Reality Check 5.15 Editor’s Note

Chapter 5 Wildlife Encounters “Between a human and a bear, the bear is not the one making poor decisions.” - Park Ranger wisdom 5.1 Introduction Wild animals are wild for a reason. They are unpredictable, powerful, and operating on instincts honed over millions of years of evolution. Humans, on the other hand, often operate on a dangerous combination of curiosity, overconfidence, and a fundamental misunderstanding of the phrase “apex predator.” This chapter collects stories from people who forgot that animals have teeth, claws, venom, and absolutely no obligation to tolerate human stupidity. 5.2 The Bear That Said No Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Story to be collected about bear encounters, feeding wildlife, getting too close for photos, or camping without proper food storage] 5.2.1 What Makes Bears Dangerous Black bears: 200-600 pounds of muscle Grizzly bears: 400-800 pounds of “you’re lunch” Polar bears: 900-1600 pounds of pure predator All bears: Faster than you, stronger than you, and not impressed by your camera Story continues… 5.3 The Snake Incident Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Story about venomous snake encounters, attempted snake handling, or “I thought it was dead” moments] Venomous snakes kill approximately 100,000 people worldwide each year. The vast majority of bites occur when someone: - Tries to kill the snake - Tries to handle the snake - Doesn’t see the snake until it’s too late - Does something monumentally stupid around the snake Our contributors fall into categories 2 and 4. 5.4 Sharks, Jellyfish, and Ocean Regrets Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Stories about marine life encounters: sharks, jellyfish, stingrays, or other aquatic animals that fought back] The ocean is not our natural habitat. Everything in it is better adapted to being there than we are. And yet, we persist in treating it like a theme park. 5.5 The Kangaroo Boxing Match (Extended) Contributor: Dave M., Queensland, Australia [Full story about attempting to fight or harass kangaroos, which are surprisingly dangerous] 5.6 Insects That Fight Back Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Stories about bee stings, wasp nests, fire ants, or other tiny creatures that punch above their weight class] 5.6.1 Why You Should Respect Insects Bees: Can kill if you’re allergic Wasps: More aggressive than bees, can sting multiple times Hornets: Like wasps, but bigger and angrier Fire ants: Tiny, numerous, and vengeful Bullet ants: Named for a reason 5.7 The Moose Incident (Continued) [See Chapter 3 for the original moose story - additional moose encounters can go here] Moose facts that should terrify you: - They kill more people in Alaska than bears - Bulls can weigh 1,500 pounds - They can run 35 mph - They are aggressive during rutting season (fall) - They don’t care about your hiking plans 5.8 When Pets Turn Wild Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Stories about “tame” wild animals, exotic pets, or domestic animals in unexpected dangerous situations] Just because you raised it from a baby doesn’t mean it’s domesticated. The following animals do NOT make good pets: - Big cats - Primates - Bears - Wolves - Venomous reptiles - Anything that can kill you 5.9 Bird Attacks Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Stories about geese, swans, cassowaries, ostriches, or other birds that attacked humans] Birds may be descendants of dinosaurs, and some of them remember this fact better than others. 5.10 The Alligator Misunderstanding Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Stories from Florida, Louisiana, or other alligator territory about getting too close to gators] If you live in alligator country, you learn certain rules: 1. Don’t swim in lakes at dawn or dusk 2. Don’t feed the alligators (it’s illegal for a reason) 3. Keep pets away from the water’s edge 4. Assume every body of water has an alligator in it Our contributor learned these rules the hard way. 5.11 Spiders and Other Arachnids Contributor: [Name - To Be Collected] [Stories about venomous spiders, including black widows, brown recluses, or Australian varieties] 5.12 Short Takes: Brief Wildlife Regrets 5.12.1 The Raccoon Incident Contributed by [Name - To Be Collected] [Story about raccoons, which are surprisingly aggressive and carry rabies] 5.12.2 The Squirrel Attack Contributed by [Name - To Be Collected] [Story about squirrels, which have teeth and no fear] 5.12.3 The Deer Kick Contributed by [Name - To Be Collected] [Story about deer, which are not as gentle as Disney suggests] 5.12.4 The Bat in the Bedroom Contributed by [Name - To Be Collected] [Story about bats, rabies testing, and why you don’t touch flying mammals] 5.13 Lessons from the Animal Kingdom What these stories teach us: Wild animals are wild - No matter how cute, how small, or how habituated to humans Teeth and claws evolved for a reason - Usually that reason is defense or predation You are not Steve Irwin - And even he had limitations Personal space applies to animals - Give them distance Food brings danger - Never feed wild animals Mothers are protective - Never get between a mother and her young Mating season is dangerous - Animals are aggressive when hormonal Fast beats slow - Most animals can outrun you Strong beats weak - Most animals are stronger than you think Respect saves lives - Yours and theirs 5.14 Statistical Reality Check According to various wildlife agencies: Hippos kill ~500 people per year (most dangerous large land mammal) Elephants kill ~500 people per year Crocodiles kill ~1,000 people per year Snakes kill ~100,000 people per year Dogs kill ~25,000 people per year (mostly through rabies) Mosquitoes kill ~700,000 people per year (through disease) For comparison: - Sharks kill ~10 people per year - Bears kill ~1-5 people per year in North America - Wolves kill <1 person per year The animals we fear are often not the ones we should fear. But we should respect all of them. 5.15 Editor’s Note This chapter is seeking contributions about: Bear encounters and near-attacks Venomous snake bites and escapes Marine animal incidents (sharks, jellyfish, stingrays, etc.) Large herbivore attacks (moose, elk, bison, hippos, elephants) Predator encounters (big cats, wolves, alligators, crocodiles) Unexpected dangerous animals (geese, swans, kangaroos, etc.) Insect swarms and stings Exotic pet incidents Rabies scares Please submit stories that include: - What animal was involved - What you did wrong - How you escaped or survived - What you learned - Whether you required medical attention Remember: We love animals. These stories are not anti-wildlife; they’re pro-common-sense. Respect wildlife from a safe distance, and you won’t have a story for this chapter. This chapter is actively collecting stories. Submissions welcome at [contact information].

Chapter 6 References and Resources About the Darwin Awards The Darwin Awards have been documenting nature’s most unfortunate examples of natural selection since the 1990s. For those interested in learning more about the “original” awards: Primary Sources DarwinAwards.com - The official Darwin Awards website, maintained by Wendy Northcutt Northcutt, Wendy. The Darwin Awards: Evolution in Action. Dutton, 2000. Northcutt, Wendy. The Darwin Awards II: Unnatural Selection. Dutton, 2001. Northcutt, Wendy. The Darwin Awards III: Survival of the Fittest. Dutton, 2003. Northcutt, Wendy. The Darwin Awards 4: Intelligent Design. Dutton, 2006. Academic Background Darwin, Charles. On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection. John Murray, 1859. Darwin, Charles. The Descent of Man, and Selection in Relation to Sex. John Murray, 1871. Safety Resources While this book is intended as entertainment, we take safety seriously. If you’re inspired to NOT win a Darwin Award yourself, these resources can help: General Safety OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration): www.osha.gov National Safety Council: www.nsc.org Red Cross First Aid and Safety: www.redcross.org Fire Safety National Fire Protection Association: www.nfpa.org U.S. Fire Administration: www.usfa.fema.gov Emergency number: 911 (US), 999 (UK), 000 (Australia), 112 (EU) Electrical Safety Electrical Safety Foundation International: www.esfi.org Always hire licensed electricians for electrical work Install GFCI outlets in bathrooms, kitchens, and outdoor areas Wildlife Safety National Park Service Wildlife Safety: www.nps.gov/subjects/wildlife/safety.htm Bear Safety Center: www.bear.org Leave No Trace: www.lnt.org Remember: A fed bear is a dead bear. A stupid human is a Darwin Award winner. Water Safety American Red Cross Water Safety: www.redcross.org/take-a-class/swimming U.S. Coast Guard Boating Safety: www.uscgboating.org Never swim alone Always wear life jackets on boats General Outdoor Safety REI Expert Advice: www.rei.com/learn/expert-advice Mountaineers Books Safety Series: Various titles Always tell someone where you’re going and when you’ll return Medical Emergency Resources If you find yourself in a situation that might qualify you for a Darwin Award: Emergency Services: 911 (US), 999 (UK), 000 (Australia), 112 (EU) Poison Control: 1-800-222-1222 (US) Crisis Text Line: Text HOME to 741741 Contributing to This Book We’re always looking for more stories of people who narrowly avoided winning a Darwin Award. If you’d like to contribute your story: Submission Guidelines You must have survived - This is non-negotiable The story must be true - We’re not collecting fiction (the truth is funny enough) You must own the rights - Don’t send us someone else’s story without permission Details can be changed - We respect anonymity and privacy Length: 500-3000 words preferred, but we’re flexible What We’re Looking For Close calls - Moments where you should have died but didn’t Lessons learned - What did this experience teach you? Honesty - Own your mistakes; we all make them Humor - If you can laugh about it now, so can we Universal themes - Stories that readers can relate to What We’re NOT Looking For Stories where others were seriously harmed Deliberate criminal activity (accidental stupidity is fine) Stories that encourage dangerous behavior Anything that would constitute a how-to guide for harm How to Submit [Contact information to be added] Email: [submissions email] Website: [project website] Social Media: [project social media handles] Acknowledgments This book would not exist without the brave (and foolish) individuals who were willing to share their most embarrassing, terrifying, and ridiculous near-death experiences. We thank you for your honesty, your humor, and your willingness to let the rest of us learn from your mistakes. Special thanks to: All contributors - Named and anonymous, you are the heart of this book Emergency responders - The paramedics, firefighters, ER doctors, and first responders who saved our contributors’ lives Concerned bystanders - Those who happened to be in the right place at the right time Patient family members - Who love our contributors despite their spectacular lapses in judgment Gravity, fire, electricity, and wildlife - For their restraint in not finishing the job Legal Disclaimer READ THIS PART: Do not attempt anything described in this book. These are cautionary tales, not instruction manuals. The stories are true to the best of our knowledge, but details have been changed to protect privacy. Names, locations, and identifying information may have been altered. We are not responsible if you read about someone touching a moose and think “I should try that.” That’s called a “Darwin Award waiting to happen.” This book is for entertainment purposes only. It is not a substitute for professional safety training, common sense, or basic survival instincts. If you’re reading this and thinking “That doesn’t sound so dangerous,” please put the book down and re-evaluate your life choices. A Final Word The line between a near-miss and a tragedy is often measured in inches, seconds, or sheer dumb luck. Every person in this book crossed that line and lived to tell the tale. We hope these stories make you laugh. We hope they make you cringe. Most of all, we hope they make you think twice before doing something spectacularly stupid. Because the difference between a good story and a Darwin Award is whether you survive to tell it. Stay safe. Stay smart. Stay alive. And remember: We did not win a Darwin Award. Let’s keep it that way. Further Reading Books About Survival and Near-Misses Ralston, Aron. Between a Rock and a Hard Place. Atria Books, 2004. Krakauer, Jon. Into Thin Air. Villard, 1997. Gonzales, Laurence. Deep Survival: Who Lives, Who Dies, and Why. W.W. Norton, 2003. Wiseman, John “Lofty.” SAS Survival Handbook. William Morrow, 2009. Books About Human Error and Stupidity Hallinan, Joseph T. Why We Make Mistakes. Broadway Books, 2009. Dobelli, Rolf. The Art of Thinking Clearly. Harper, 2013. Kahneman, Daniel. Thinking, Fast and Slow. Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011. Humor About Death and Danger Roach, Mary. Stiff: The Curious Lives of Human Cadavers. W.W. Norton, 2003. The Darwin Awards series (see above) The Worst-Case Scenario Survival Handbook series. Chronicle Books, various years. Colophon This book was created using: R Markdown and bookdown by Yihui Xie RStudio for editing and compilation Pandoc for document conversion LaTeX for PDF generation Numerous cups of coffee and nervous laughter For technical questions about the bookdown format, see: Xie, Yihui. bookdown: Authoring Books and Technical Documents with R Markdown. Chapman and Hall/CRC, 2016. Online documentation: https://bookdown.org Version: 1.0 (Draft) Last Updated: 2026-04-14 Status: Collecting stories and contributions This book is dedicated to everyone who has ever done something breathtakingly stupid and lived to regret it. You are not alone. You are in excellent company. And most importantly, you are still alive to read this. May you continue to not win a Darwin Award.
